  • 3. Simple OR: | → when_any()
    • Old Way
    • New Way

  • 4. Value Recoding: case_when() → recode_values()
    • Old Way
    • New Way
    • Why Better?

    • Old Way
    • New Way
    • Why Better?

    • Old Way
    • New Way
    • Why Better?
  • 7. Simple Value Replacement: case_when() → replace_values()
    • Old Way
    • New Way
    • Why Better?
  • 8. Error on Unmatched: case_when() → recode_values()
    • Old Way
    • New Way
    • Why Better?

  • Installation

dplyr 1.2.0 Conversion Cheatsheet

From Old Patterns to New Functions

library(dplyr)
Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.4.3
library(palmerpenguins)

This cheatsheet walks through converting old dplyr patterns to the new dplyr 1.2.0 functions. Each section shows the old way and the new way side-by-side.

1. Dropping Rows: filter() → filter_out()

Old Way

# Using ! negation
penguins |>
  filter(!(species == "Adelie")) |>
  count(species)
# A tibble: 2 × 2
  species       n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Chinstrap    68
2 Gentoo      124

New Way

# Clear intent: drop these rows
penguins |>
  filter_out(species == "Adelie") |>
  count(species)
# A tibble: 2 × 2
  species       n
  <fct>     <int>
1 Chinstrap    68
2 Gentoo      124

Why Better?

  • ✅ Clear intent (drop vs keep)
  • ✅ Better NA handling (keeps NAs by default)
  • ✅ No complex negation logic needed

2. OR Logic: | → when_any()

Old Way

# Multiple | operators
penguins |>
  filter(
    (species == "Adelie" & island == "Torgersen" & body_mass_g > 3700) |
    (species == "Gentoo" & body_mass_g > 5000)
  ) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)
# A tibble: 83 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 4 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        4400
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4200
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        4450
10 Adelie  Torgersen        3900
# ℹ 73 more rows

New Way

# Clean OR logic with when_any()
penguins |>
  filter(
    when_any(
      when_all(species == "Adelie", island == "Torgersen", body_mass_g > 3700),
      when_all(species == "Gentoo", body_mass_g > 5000)
    )
  ) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)
# A tibble: 83 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 4 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        4400
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        4500
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4200
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        4450
10 Adelie  Torgersen        3900
# ℹ 73 more rows

Why Better?

  • ✅ Explicit OR logic (no need to mentally parse |)
  • ✅ Nests cleanly with when_all()
  • ✅ Easier to add/remove conditions

3. Simple OR: | → when_any()

Old Way

# Simple OR with |
penguins |>
  filter(species == "Adelie" | body_mass_g > 5000) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)
# A tibble: 213 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3450
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        3650
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        3625
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        3475
10 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
# ℹ 203 more rows

New Way

# Explicit OR
penguins |>
  filter(when_any(species == "Adelie", body_mass_g > 5000)) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g)
# A tibble: 213 × 3
   species island    body_mass_g
   <fct>   <fct>           <int>
 1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
 2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
 3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
 4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA
 5 Adelie  Torgersen        3450
 6 Adelie  Torgersen        3650
 7 Adelie  Torgersen        3625
 8 Adelie  Torgersen        4675
 9 Adelie  Torgersen        3475
10 Adelie  Torgersen        4250
# ℹ 203 more rows

Why Better?

  • ✅ Clearer intent (especially for newcomers)
  • ✅ Consistent with nested when_any() patterns

4. Value Recoding: case_when() → recode_values()

Old Way

# Repetitive == checks
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      island == "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
      island == "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest",
      .default = island
    )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northwest      
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northwest      
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northwest

New Way

# Direct value mapping
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = island |>
      recode_values(
        "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
        "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
        "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest"
      )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northwest      
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northwest      
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northwest

Why Better?

  • ✅ No repetitive == checks
  • ✅ Clearer intent (recoding values)
  • ✅ Can use lookup tables
  • ✅ Better error handling with unmatched = "error"

5. Using Lookup Tables: case_when() → recode_values()

Old Way

# Manual case_when from vectors
island_mapping <- list(
  "Biscoe" = "Southwest",
  "Dream" = "Northwest",
  "Torgersen" = "Northwest"
)

# Have to construct case_when manually
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe" ~ island_mapping[["Biscoe"]],
      island == "Dream" ~ island_mapping[["Dream"]],
      island == "Torgersen" ~ island_mapping[["Torgersen"]],
      .default = island
    )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northwest      
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northwest      
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northwest

New Way

# Use tibble directly as lookup
island_mapping_tibble <- tibble(
  from = c("Biscoe", "Dream", "Torgersen"),
  to = c("Southwest", "Northwest", "Northwest")
)

penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = island |>
      recode_values(
        from = island_mapping_tibble$from,
        to = island_mapping_tibble$to
      )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>          
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northwest      
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northwest      
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northwest      
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northwest

Why Better?

  • ✅ Lookup tables stored as data frames
  • ✅ Easy to maintain and update mappings
  • ✅ Can load from CSV or database

6. Conditional Replacement: case_when() → replace_when()

Old Way

# Need .default for same column
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    body_mass_g = case_when(
      body_mass_g > 5000 ~ 5000,
      .default = body_mass_g  # Boilerplate!
    )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA

New Way

# No .default needed!
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    body_mass_g = replace_when(
      body_mass_g,
      body_mass_g > 5000 ~ 5000
    )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 3
  species island    body_mass_g
  <fct>   <fct>           <int>
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA

Why Better?

  • ✅ No .default boilerplate
  • ✅ Clear intent (replacing specific values)
  • ✅ Unmatched values automatically preserved

7. Simple Value Replacement: case_when() → replace_values()

Old Way

# Need .default even for exact match
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      island == "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
      island == "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest"
    )
  ) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = case_when(
      island_location == "Northwest" ~ "Northern Palmer Archipelago",
      .default = island_location
    )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location            
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>                      
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northern Palmer Archipelago
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northern Palmer Archipelago
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northern Palmer Archipelago
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northern Palmer Archipelago

New Way

# Direct replacement
penguins |>
  slice(1:4) |>
  select(species, island, body_mass_g) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = island |>
      recode_values(
        "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
        "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
        "Torgersen" ~ "Northwest"
      )
  ) |>
  mutate(
    island_location = island_location |>
      replace_values(
        "Northwest" ~ "Northern Palmer Archipelago"
      )
  )
# A tibble: 4 × 4
  species island    body_mass_g island_location            
  <fct>   <fct>           <int> <chr>                      
1 Adelie  Torgersen        3750 Northern Palmer Archipelago
2 Adelie  Torgersen        3800 Northern Palmer Archipelago
3 Adelie  Torgersen        3250 Northern Palmer Archipelago
4 Adelie  Torgersen          NA Northern Palmer Archipelago

Why Better?

  • ✅ No .default needed
  • ✅ Clear that only specific values are being replaced
  • ✅ Works with any number of replacements

8. Error on Unmatched: case_when() → recode_values()

Old Way

# Manual validation needed
islands <- c("Biscoe", "Dream", "UnknownIsland")
data <- tibble(island = islands)

# case_when() silently returns NA for unmatched
result <- data |>
  mutate(
    location = case_when(
      island == "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
      island == "Dream" ~ "Northwest"
      # UnknownIsland becomes NA - no error!
    )
  )

print(result)
# A tibble: 3 × 2
  island        location 
  <chr>         <chr>    
1 Biscoe        Southwest
2 Dream         Northwest
3 UnknownIsland <NA>     
# Would need to manually check for NAs
if (any(is.na(result$location))) {
  stop("Unmatched values found!")
}
Error: Unmatched values found!

New Way

# Automatic validation
islands <- c("Biscoe", "Dream", "UnknownIsland")
data <- tibble(island = islands)

# Errors immediately on unmatched value
data |>
  mutate(
    location = island |>
      recode_values(
        "Biscoe" ~ "Southwest",
        "Dream" ~ "Northwest",
        unmatched = "error"  # Explicit error handling
      )
  )
Error in `mutate()`:
ℹ In argument: `location = recode_values(...)`.
Caused by error in `recode_values()`:
! Each location must be matched.
✖ Location 3 is unmatched.

Why Better?

  • ✅ Fail fast on data issues
  • ✅ Explicit error handling
  • ✅ No silent NA creation

Quick Reference Table

Pattern Old Way New Way Key Benefit
Drop rows filter(!(x)) filter_out(x) Clear intent, better NA handling
OR logic a \| b when_any(a, b) Explicit, nests cleanly
AND logic a & b when_all(a, b) Explicit, nests with when_any()
Recode values case_when(x == "a" ~ "A", ...) recode_values("a" ~ "A", ...) No repetitive ==
Use lookup table Manual case_when() recode_values(from = ..., to = ...) Direct data frame usage
Replace values conditionally case_when(..., .default = x) replace_when(x, ...) No .default boilerplate
Replace specific values case_when(x == "a" ~ "A", .default = x) replace_values("a" ~ "A") No .default boilerplate
Error on unmatched Manual validation recode_values(..., unmatched = "error") Fail fast

Rules of Thumb

Use filter() to keep rows

Use filter_out() to drop rows (if you’re using ! in filter())

Use when_any() for OR logic (if you have complex | expressions)

Use when_all() for AND logic (useful when nesting inside when_any())

Use recode_values() for exact matches (if you’re using == in case_when())

Use replace_when() to modify existing columns (instead of case_when() with .default)

Use replace_values() for simple replacements (instead of case_when() with .default)

Installation

install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("dplyr")

Current version: dplyr 1.2.0

Generated for dplyr 1.2.0 - Working smarter with new functions
